title: The Other Town's Mirror Channel

features.name: town-marker-west
features.desc-on-touch: A small cottage claiming to be the original town; its register dates back, but the river has changed its course through here four times; is this the same place or does sameness require the river to stay still?

features.name: town-marker-east
features.desc-on-touch: A cottage identical to its western mirror; locals claim both towns are equally true; the river disagrees by flowing through both; argument becomes hydrography.

features.name: river-spine
features.desc-on-touch: Water that flows east and west simultaneously; children wade in it and age backward in one direction, forward in the other; time branches at the current.

features.name: dual-records
features.desc-on-touch: Stacked histories in two colours: what the east town remembers and what the west town remembers; the archive holds both simultaneously; both versions are dated 1863.

features.name: mirror-signpost
features.desc-on-touch: A plaque that reads in both directions; its text says 'toward the other town' no matter which way you face; arrival and departure are the same direction.

features.name: field-between
features.desc-on-touch: You stand in grass that belongs to both towns equally; locals meet here and argue which side they're standing on; the field refuses to choose; it is the most stable ground in the region.

inscription: the water flows both directions at once; both towns read themselves in the river; neither history settles for less than total accuracy

file.name: Double_Channel_Map_Both_Towns_Simultaneously_1-50000.svg
file.note: cartographic impossibility: one river flowing through two towns that share no geography; the corruption made them equally real in the same space; surveyors kept producing different maps; all are archi

exits.label: back toward the double channel where this mirror begins
toward a third town that appears only in corruption, glimpsed between the two
to the parallel field where children are always leaving both versions of home
down into the river where both currents finally choose a direction
